cases.title: Mixed HAGMA and metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: 44M in ED with several days of prolonged vomiting and poor intake after a recent febrile GI illness. Looks tired and dry, still nauseated, but no focal abdominal findings on exam.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

options: Mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis and metabolic alkalosis

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis and metabolic alkalosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 23 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 42.5 mmHg (acceptable range 40.5-44.5). The measured PaCO2 is 42.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (90 + 23) = 26.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 26.0, so in a pure raised-gap acidosis the bicarbonate would usually be around 10.0 mmol/L. Instead it is 23 mmol/L, preserved by about 13.0 mmol/L. That delta-gap pattern reveals a second metabolic process: a concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This mixed metabolic picture reflects multiple underlying processes, such as acid generation combined with vomiting or diuretic use. The presence of more than one disturbance should prompt a broader clinical search rather than a single unifying diagnosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis with a higher-than-expected bicarbonate indicates a concurrent alkalosis. When the numbers don’t align, there is always another process.

cases.title: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: 58F brought to ED unwell with tachypnoea and diffuse abdominal discomfort. RR is up, and she looks more breathless than expected for the exam findings.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

options: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 26.1 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 21.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.2-23.2). The measured HCO3 is 13.8 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (103 + 13.8) = 25.2, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: For this degree of respiratory alkalosis, the bicarbonate would be expected to be about 21.2 mmol/L, but it is 13.8 mmol/L here. That extra fall of about 7.4 mmol/L is too large for compensation alone, and with an anion gap of 25.2 it points to an added high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern reflects significant underlying illness with two concurrent processes. A normal or near-normal pH should not reassure, as a serious metabolic acidosis may be masked by hyperventilation. This commonly occurs in conditions such as sepsis, where early respiratory changes precede clinical deterioration.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If a low PaCO2 does not fully explain the acid-base picture, use the anion gap to uncover an added metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes, missed her insulin for a couple of days and now has persistent vomiting with deep breathing. In ED she looks tachypnoeic and is still complaining of upper abdominal pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 27.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (91 + 13) = 32.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: With an anion gap of 32.0, a pure high anion gap metabolic acidosis would be expected to lower the bicarbonate to about 4.0 mmol/L. Here the bicarbonate is 13 mmol/L, so it is preserved by about 9.0 mmol/L rather than falling in step with the gap. That delta-gap mismatch points to an additional metabolic alkalosis, which is offsetting the ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 51M in ED with 3/7 of poor oral intake and recent weight loss, now feeling weak and tired. No vomiting or fever reported, and he looks otherwise well on first review.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 19 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 5.3 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 36.5 mmHg (acceptable range 34.5-38.5). The measured PaCO2 is 36.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (103 + 19) = 17.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 65F with a history of COPD is brought to ED for worsening breathlessness over the last few days. She is a smoker with chronic wheeze and reduced exercise tolerance, and is a bit drowsy on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 66 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 34.4 mmol/L (acceptable range 32.4-36.4). The measured HCO3 is 34.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently elevated PaCO₂ with renal compensation, resulting in increased bicarbonate and a near-normal pH. This reflects a stable, compensated respiratory acidosis due to long-standing ventilatory limitation.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 55M in ED with a few days of productive cough and increasing breathlessness. He’s tachypnoeic and febrile, with reduced GCS noted on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 28.1 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 21.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.6-23.6). The measured HCO3 is 21.0 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis increases respiratory drive through inflammatory and metabolic signals, leading to hyperventilation and a fall in PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis often occurs early in the disease process before metabolic abnormalities develop.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 55F brought to ED with confusion and blurred vision after a possible ingestion at home; family found a suspicious solvent container nearby. She also reports upper abdominal pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 8 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 1.2 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 20.0 mmHg (acceptable range 18.0-22.0). The measured PaCO2 is 20.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (90 + 8) = 40.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 27F with type 1 diabetes presents to ED after missing insulin for 2 days, with polyuria and nausea. She’s tachypnoeic with deep breathing on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 8 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 32.4 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 20.0 mmHg (acceptable range 18.0-22.0). The measured PaCO2 is 19.9 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 133 - (95 + 8) = 30.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 28-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes brought to ED after several episodes of vomiting overnight. She’s been missed insulin and is breathing deeply on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 19 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 36.5 mmHg (acceptable range 34.5-38.5). The measured PaCO2 is 36.9 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (94 + 19) = 27.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: With an anion gap of 27.0, a pure high anion gap metabolic acidosis would be expected to lower the bicarbonate to about 9.0 mmol/L. Here the bicarbonate is 19 mmol/L, so it is preserved by about 10.0 mmol/L rather than falling in step with the gap. That delta-gap mismatch points to an additional metabolic alkalosis, which is offsetting the ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 28.4 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 21.7 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.7-23.7). The measured HCO3 is 13.8 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (92 + 13.8) = 31.2, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Here the bicarbonate is 13.8 mmol/L, lower than the roughly 21.7 mmol/L expected for isolated respiratory alkalosis. With an anion gap of 31.2, that extra drop is best explained by a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis. In salicylate toxicity this is a true mixed primary disorder, not just a compensatory response.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is characteristic of salicylate toxicity, where early hyperventilation is followed by metabolic acidosis. The combination should prompt urgent consideration of overdose, even if the history is unclear. Early recognition is critical, as clinical deterioration can be rapid.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate cases are high-risk because the alkalosis and acidosis co-exist, so compensation formulas help expose the mixed picture quickly.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 41M brought to ED with confusion and blurred vision after a possible ingestion. Family found a suspicious solvent container at home, and he also reports some epigastric pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 9 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 1.5 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 21.5 mmHg (acceptable range 19.5-23.5). The measured PaCO2 is 21.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 144 - (95 + 9) = 40.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 26F in ED after a recent alcohol binge with reduced oral intake for 3 days. She’s been vomiting and looks dry on exam, with mild tachypnoea.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 16 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 32.0 mmHg (acceptable range 30.0-34.0). The measured PaCO2 is 32.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (96 + 16) = 24.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: ED review of a 24-year-old man with persistent vomiting since overnight, now dry on exam with a bit of epigastric discomfort. He’s alert but looks tired, and an ABG was sent.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 35 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Metabolic alkalosis compensation, the expected PaCO2 is about 47.7 mmHg (acceptable range 44.7-50.7). The measured PaCO2 is 47.4 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, leading to a metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and increased pH. The body compensates by hypoventilating to retain CO₂, raising PaCO₂. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help maintain the alkalosis, so recognising this pattern is important as correction requires replacement of both volume and chloride.

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.39, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 23.2 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 20.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.6-22.6). The measured HCO3 is 13.5 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (102 + 13.5) = 23.5, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: For this degree of respiratory alkalosis, the bicarbonate would be expected to be about 20.6 mmol/L, but it is 13.5 mmol/L here. That extra fall of about 7.1 mmol/L is too large for compensation alone, and with an anion gap of 23.5 it points to an added high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern reflects significant underlying illness with two concurrent processes. A normal or near-normal pH should not reassure, as a serious metabolic acidosis may be masked by hyperventilation. This commonly occurs in conditions such as sepsis, where early respiratory changes precede clinical deterioration.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If a low PaCO2 does not fully explain the acid-base picture, use the anion gap to uncover an added metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 26 year old male in ED with rapid breathing after a panic episode, complaining of tingling in both hands and feeling light-headed. He is anxious and speaking in short phrases.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 32 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits acute hyperventilation such as panic.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Hyperventilation increases alveolar ventilation, leading to excessive loss of CO₂. As PaCO₂ falls, hydrogen ion concentration decreases, causing a rise in pH and a primary respiratory alkalosis. This disturbance is driven by increased breathing rather than a metabolic process, with common triggers including anxiety or panic, pain, hypoxia, and systemic illness such as sepsis.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 29M presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss, leading to a metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 26.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (118 + 13) = 11.0, which is normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct loss of bicarbonate from the GI tract. This results in a hyperchloraemic acidosis as chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality. The pattern is typically accompanied by volume depletion and may be associated with low potassium. Recognising this mechanism helps distinguish it from renal causes of bicarbonate loss and from high anion gap acidoses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The acidosis fits a normal anion gap pattern, but think bicarbonate loss from the gut rather than unmeasured acids.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60M brought to ED in shock with cold peripheries and mottled skin, BP low and HR up. He looks unwell and is tachypnoeic, with an ABG sent on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 11.3 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 8.3 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 25.0 mmHg (acceptable range 23.0-27.0). The measured PaCO2 is 24.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (98 + 11.3) = 32.7, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 68.9 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory acidosis, buffering and early renal compensation raise HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 26.9 mmol/L (acceptable range 24.9-28.9). The measured HCO3 is 21.9 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (98 + 21.9) = 21.1, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Respiratory acidosis should produce a compensatory rise in bicarbonate to about 26.9 mmol/L, but the measured bicarbonate is only 21.9 mmol/L. That shortfall of about 5.0 mmol/L, together with an anion gap of 21.1, supports an additional high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern suggests severe physiological compromise, with both impaired ventilation and a concurrent metabolic acidosis. It is often seen in critically unwell patients and indicates a high risk of deterioration. Prompt assessment and escalation of care are essential.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When respiratory acidosis and a raised anion gap coexist, expected compensation helps confirm that two primary processes are present.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 48M presents to ED with nausea and vomiting after a recent alcohol binge. He’s had minimal intake for the last couple of days and looks unwell on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0). The measured PaCO2 is 26.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 135 - (97 + 12) = 26.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 29M in ED with 3/7 of poor oral intake and fatigue, with recent weight loss. He looks mildly dehydrated on exam and says he’s been fasting.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 16 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 4.8 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 32.0 mmHg (acceptable range 30.0-34.0). The measured PaCO2 is 32.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (101 + 16) = 21.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Metabolic alkalosis compensation, the expected PaCO2 is about 47.0 mmHg (acceptable range 44.0-50.0). The measured PaCO2 is 47.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, leading to a metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and increased pH. The body compensates by hypoventilating to retain CO₂, raising PaCO₂. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help maintain the alkalosis, so recognising this pattern is important as correction requires replacement of both volume and chloride.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.36, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 23 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is a mixed high anion gap metabolic acidosis with concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 42.5 mmHg (acceptable range 40.5-44.5). The measured PaCO2 is 42.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (90 + 23) = 25.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: The anion gap is 25.0, so in a pure raised-gap acidosis the bicarbonate would usually be around 11.0 mmol/L. Instead it is 23 mmol/L, preserved by about 12.0 mmol/L. That delta-gap pattern reveals a second metabolic process: a concurrent metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This mixed metabolic picture reflects multiple underlying processes, such as acid generation combined with vomiting or diuretic use. The presence of more than one disturbance should prompt a broader clinical search rather than a single unifying diagnosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis with a higher-than-expected bicarbonate indicates a concurrent alkalosis. When the numbers don’t align, there is always another process.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 72F with COPD in ED with worsening breathlessness and a productive cough with increased sputum. She’s a bit drowsy on review, with reduced air entry and wheeze.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 85.7 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 42.3 mmol/L (acceptable range 40.3-44.3). The measured HCO3 is 38.8 mmol/L. This is outside the expected range, so the metabolic compensation is inappropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation causes worsening ventilatory failure, leading to a rise in PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. Renal compensation has not had time to adjust, so the bicarbonate does not increase appropriately. This represents an acute deterioration on a background of chronic respiratory disease.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 38-year-old woman in ED after taking a large quantity of aspirin, with tinnitus, diaphoresis, and marked hyperventilation. She also reports nausea and upper abdominal discomfort, and an ABG has been sent.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

options: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 26.0 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 21.2 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.2-23.2). The measured HCO3 is 13.2 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (93 + 13.2) = 30.8, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Here the bicarbonate is 13.2 mmol/L, lower than the roughly 21.2 mmol/L expected for isolated respiratory alkalosis. With an anion gap of 30.8, that extra drop is best explained by a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis. In salicylate toxicity this is a true mixed primary disorder, not just a compensatory response.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is characteristic of salicylate toxicity, where early hyperventilation is followed by metabolic acidosis. The combination should prompt urgent consideration of overdose, even if the history is unclear. Early recognition is critical, as clinical deterioration can be rapid.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate cases are high-risk because the alkalosis and acidosis co-exist, so compensation formulas help expose the mixed picture quickly.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 36M in ED after a recent alcohol binge with poor oral intake and a couple of days of nausea and vomiting. He looks dehydrated with dry mucous membranes and mild tachypnoea.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 8 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 20.0 mmHg (acceptable range 18.0-22.0). The measured PaCO2 is 19.9 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (96 + 8) = 32.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 20 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss, leading to a metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 38.0 mmHg (acceptable range 36.0-40.0). The measured PaCO2 is 37.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (106 + 20) = 10.0, which is normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct loss of bicarbonate from the GI tract. This results in a hyperchloraemic acidosis as chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality. The pattern is typically accompanied by volume depletion and may be associated with low potassium. Recognising this mechanism helps distinguish it from renal causes of bicarbonate loss and from high anion gap acidoses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The acidosis fits a normal anion gap pattern, but think bicarbonate loss from the gut rather than unmeasured acids.

cases.title: Simple metabolic alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 29 year old female at 10 weeks gestation presents with severe nausea and frequent vomiting. She reports poor oral intake and dizziness. A blood gas is taken.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple metabolic alkalosis pattern with elevated bicarbonate and compensatory CO2 retention

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 31 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Metabolic alkalosis occurs when there is a relative excess of bicarbonate, usually due to loss of hydrogen ions or contraction of extracellular fluid volume. As bicarbonate rises, chloride often falls to maintain electroneutrality. A common cause is loss of gastric acid through excessive vomiting.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 34F in ED with type 1 diabetes, says she missed insulin for the last day and has had polyuria since yesterday. She’s tachypnoeic with deep breathing on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 9 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 28.6 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 21.5 mmHg (acceptable range 19.5-23.5). The measured PaCO2 is 21.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (100 + 9) = 30.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins. The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis. Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause. A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: 44F in ED, unwell with tachypnoea and diffuse abdominal discomfort. RR is high and she looks unsettled, but no obvious wheeze or chest findings on initial review.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 23.6 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 20.7 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.7-22.7). The measured HCO3 is 12.6 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (102 + 12.6) = 23.4, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: For this degree of respiratory alkalosis, the bicarbonate would be expected to be about 20.7 mmol/L, but it is 12.6 mmol/L here. That extra fall of about 8.1 mmol/L is too large for compensation alone, and with an anion gap of 23.4 it points to an added high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern reflects significant underlying illness with two concurrent processes. A normal or near-normal pH should not reassure, as a serious metabolic acidosis may be masked by hyperventilation. This commonly occurs in conditions such as sepsis, where early respiratory changes precede clinical deterioration.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If a low PaCO2 does not fully explain the acid-base picture, use the anion gap to uncover an added metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 19-year-old man with type 1 diabetes, has missed insulin for a few days and reports polyuria. In ED he’s tachypnoeic with deep breathing but still alert.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 27.1 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 27.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (100 + 13) = 25.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 28F with type 1 diabetes presents to ED with 3/7 vomiting and increased work of breathing. She says she missed her insulin over the last day and now feels weak.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0). The measured PaCO2 is 25.9 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (96 + 12) = 29.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: With an anion gap of 29.0, a pure high anion gap metabolic acidosis would be expected to lower the bicarbonate to about 7.0 mmol/L. Here the bicarbonate is 12 mmol/L, so it is preserved by about 5.0 mmol/L rather than falling in step with the gap. That delta-gap mismatch points to an additional metabolic alkalosis, which is offsetting the ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 68M with known COPD and a long smoking history presents with worsening breathlessness over the past few days. He’s been coughing up more sputum than usual and is working harder to breathe in ED.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 85.0 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 42.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 40.0-44.0). The measured HCO3 is 38.3 mmol/L. This is outside the expected range, so the metabolic compensation is inappropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation causes worsening ventilatory failure, leading to a rise in PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. Renal compensation has not had time to adjust, so the bicarbonate does not increase appropriately. This represents an acute deterioration on a background of chronic respiratory disease.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65F from home brought in by ambulance. She is hypotensive and tachycardic on arrival. She's had abdominal pain since last night and appears shocked.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13.3 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 5.6 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 28.0 mmHg (acceptable range 26.0-30.0). The measured PaCO2 is 28.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (97 + 13.3) = 29.7, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72M in ED with a few days of dizziness and fatigue after a recent increase in his diuretics. He looks a bit dry on exam and says he has been taking his usual tablets regularly.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 36 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Metabolic alkalosis compensation, the expected PaCO2 is about 48.4 mmHg (acceptable range 45.4-51.4). The measured PaCO2 is 46.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 72M brought in by ambulance with abdominal pain and hypotension, now tachycardic and cold peripheries. He looks unwell and has been in shock since arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12.9 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 8.0 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.4 mmHg (acceptable range 25.4-29.4). The measured PaCO2 is 28.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (98 + 12.9) = 31.1, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 30 year old female presents to ED after almost being struck by a car. She is anxious, has chest tightness and rapid breathing. Oxygen saturations are normal.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 28 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits acute hyperventilation such as panic.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Hyperventilation increases alveolar ventilation, leading to excessive loss of CO₂. As PaCO₂ falls, hydrogen ion concentration decreases, causing a rise in pH and a primary respiratory alkalosis. This disturbance is driven by increased breathing rather than a metabolic process, with common triggers including anxiety or panic, pain, hypoxia, and systemic illness such as sepsis.
