cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65F brought in by ambulance with hypotension and a cold, mottled peripheries. She’s tachycardic and looks unwell, with an ABG sent in ED.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 10.9 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 7.4 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 24.4 mmHg (acceptable range 22.4-26.4). The measured PaCO2 is 24.3 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (100 + 10.9) = 30.1, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a high anion gap, but compensation still needs to be checked before calling the picture isolated uraemia.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 76M in ED with dizziness and fatigue after a recent increase in his thiazide dose. Mildly dry on exam, no vomiting reported.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 36 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Metabolic alkalosis compensation, the expected PaCO2 is about 48.4 mmHg (acceptable range 45.4-51.4). The measured PaCO2 is 46.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 21.7 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 20.3 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.3-22.3). The measured HCO3 is 17.5 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (98 + 17.5) = 21.5, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Here the bicarbonate is 17.5 mmol/L, lower than the roughly 20.3 mmol/L expected for isolated respiratory alkalosis. With an anion gap of 21.5, that extra drop is best explained by a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis. In salicylate toxicity this is a true mixed primary disorder, not just a compensatory response.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is characteristic of salicylate toxicity, where early hyperventilation is followed by metabolic acidosis. The combination should prompt urgent consideration of overdose, even if the history is unclear. Early recognition is critical, as clinical deterioration can be rapid.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate cases are high-risk because the alkalosis and acidosis co-exist, so compensation formulas help expose the mixed picture quickly.

cases.title: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: 68-year-old man in ED with worsening breathlessness and increasing drowsiness. He’s tachypnoeic but hard to rouse, with no clear story of overdose.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

options: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 64.4 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 33.8 mmol/L (acceptable range 31.8-35.8). The measured HCO3 is 24.7 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (91 + 24.7) = 22.3, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Respiratory acidosis should produce a compensatory rise in bicarbonate to about 33.8 mmol/L, but the measured bicarbonate is only 24.7 mmol/L. That shortfall of about 9.1 mmol/L, together with an anion gap of 22.3, supports an additional high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern suggests severe physiological compromise, with both impaired ventilation and a concurrent metabolic acidosis. It is often seen in critically unwell patients and indicates a high risk of deterioration. Prompt assessment and escalation of care are essential.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When respiratory acidosis and a raised anion gap coexist, expected compensation helps confirm that two primary processes are present.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 62 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chronic CO2 retention in the setting of COPD.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation

explanation_blueprint.body: Chronic CO₂ retention in COPD leads to a persistently elevated PaCO₂ with renal compensation, resulting in increased bicarbonate and a near-normal pH. This reflects a stable, compensated respiratory acidosis due to long-standing ventilatory limitation.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 68M with known COPD and a smoking history presents with worsening breathlessness over 3 days. He has a productive cough with increased sputum and is more drowsy than usual.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 85.2 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 42.1 mmol/L (acceptable range 40.1-44.1). The measured HCO3 is 37.9 mmol/L. This is outside the expected range, so the metabolic compensation is inappropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation causes worsening ventilatory failure, leading to a rise in PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. Renal compensation has not had time to adjust, so the bicarbonate does not increase appropriately. This represents an acute deterioration on a background of chronic respiratory disease.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 34M brought to ED after possible ingestion of a suspicious substance found beside a solvent container. He is confused and reports blurred vision, with some epigastric discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 1.6 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 29.0 mmHg (acceptable range 27.0-31.0). The measured PaCO2 is 28.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 141 - (104 + 14) = 23.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 30.5 mmHg (acceptable range 28.5-32.5). The measured PaCO2 is 31.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (113 + 15) = 9.0, which is normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct loss of bicarbonate from the GI tract. This results in a hyperchloraemic acidosis as chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality. The pattern is typically accompanied by volume depletion and may be associated with low potassium. Recognising this mechanism helps distinguish it from renal causes of bicarbonate loss and from high anion gap acidoses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The acidosis fits a normal anion gap pattern, but think bicarbonate loss from the gut rather than unmeasured acids.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 28F with type 1 diabetes, brought to ED after several episodes of vomiting and missed insulin yesterday. She’s tachypnoeic with deep breathing and has dry mucous membranes.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This fits DKA with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis from vomiting rather than an isolated ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 29.0 mmHg (acceptable range 27.0-31.0). The measured PaCO2 is 29.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 135 - (90 + 14) = 31.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: With an anion gap of 31.0, a pure high anion gap metabolic acidosis would be expected to lower the bicarbonate to about 5.0 mmol/L. Here the bicarbonate is 14 mmol/L, so it is preserved by about 9.0 mmol/L rather than falling in step with the gap. That delta-gap mismatch points to an additional metabolic alkalosis, which is offsetting the ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency drives ketone production, causing a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis consistent with DKA. However, concurrent vomiting leads to loss of gastric acid and a superimposed metabolic alkalosis, which can partially offset the acidaemia and result in a higher-than-expected bicarbonate. This mixed picture may make the acidosis appear less severe than expected for the degree of hyperglycaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A preserved bicarbonate in DKA should prompt a search for a concurrent alkalosis rather than reassurance that the acidosis is mild.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65M with COPD and a smoking history presents to ED with worsening breathlessness over a few days and increased productive cough with thicker sputum.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 89.0 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with an acute on chronic respiratory acidosis. Chronic CO2 retention leads to metabolic compensation, with an acute rise in the PaCO2 during exacerbation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 43.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 41.6-45.6). The measured HCO3 is 38.9 mmol/L. This is outside the expected range, so the metabolic compensation is inappropriate.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: An acute COPD exacerbation causes worsening ventilatory failure, leading to a rise in PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. Renal compensation has not had time to adjust, so the bicarbonate does not increase appropriately. This represents an acute deterioration on a background of chronic respiratory disease.

cases.title: Simple normal anion gap metabolic acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 31F presents to ED with several days of diarrhoea and reduced oral intake. Her child is currently unwell with similar symptoms. She is light-headed and tired. Looks dry on arrival but has not had vomiting or abdominal pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple bicarbonate-driven metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 18 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss, leading to a metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Metabolic acidosis can occur when bicarbonate is lost from the body or when the kidneys are unable to excrete acid effectively. As bicarbonate falls, chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, which is why the chloride may be elevated. A common cause of this pattern is bicarbonate loss through the gastrointestinal tract, such as with diarrhoea.

cases.title: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: ED review of a 47-year-old woman who is unwell with tachypnoea and seems more short of breath than usual. She’s alert but speaking in short phrases, with no obvious wheeze on initial exam.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

options: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.35, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 27.5 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 21.5 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.5-23.5). The measured HCO3 is 14.6 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (100 + 14.6) = 22.4, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: For this degree of respiratory alkalosis, the bicarbonate would be expected to be about 21.5 mmol/L, but it is 14.6 mmol/L here. That extra fall of about 6.9 mmol/L is too large for compensation alone, and with an anion gap of 22.4 it points to an added high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern reflects significant underlying illness with two concurrent processes. A normal or near-normal pH should not reassure, as a serious metabolic acidosis may be masked by hyperventilation. This commonly occurs in conditions such as sepsis, where early respiratory changes precede clinical deterioration.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If a low PaCO2 does not fully explain the acid-base picture, use the anion gap to uncover an added metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 30.8 mmol/L (acceptable range 28.8-32.8). The measured HCO3 is 24.5 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 137 - (94 + 24.5) = 18.5, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Respiratory acidosis should produce a compensatory rise in bicarbonate to about 30.8 mmol/L, but the measured bicarbonate is only 24.5 mmol/L. explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern suggests severe physiological compromise, with both impaired ventilation and a concurrent metabolic acidosis. It is often seen in critically unwell patients and indicates a high risk of deterioration. Prompt assessment and escalation of care are essential.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When respiratory acidosis and a raised anion gap coexist, expected compensation helps confirm that two primary processes are present.

cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 29F in ED with 3/7 of poor oral intake after a recent viral illness, and she’s had ongoing weight loss and fatigue. Mucous membranes look dry, but obs are otherwise fairly settled.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 4.6 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 29.0 mmHg (acceptable range 27.0-31.0). The measured PaCO2 is 28.8 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 134 - (99 + 14) = 21.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 42F in ED after a recent alcohol binge with several days of poor oral intake and repeated vomiting. She looks dehydrated with dry mucous membranes and is tachypnoeic on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 10 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 23.0 mmHg (acceptable range 21.0-25.0). The measured PaCO2 is 22.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (97 + 10) = 29.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: ED handover on a 68-year-old man with advanced kidney disease and reduced urine output over the past day. He’s been increasingly weak and nauseated, with some drowsiness noted by family.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is most consistent with uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis from renal failure.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0). The measured PaCO2 is 26.1 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 135 - (96 + 12) = 27.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Uraemia causes a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of unmeasured acids from impaired renal excretion. The anion gap rises as these acids accumulate, and bicarbonate falls accordingly. Respiratory compensation lowers PaCO₂, but the degree of acidosis reflects the severity of renal dysfunction.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Renal failure can explain a high anion gap, but compensation still needs to be checked before calling the picture isolated uraemia.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 31M with type 1 diabetes presents to ED after missing insulin for the past day. He’s had polyuria and now looks tired, with noticeable tachypnoea and deep breathing on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 12 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 25.0 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 26.0 mmHg (acceptable range 24.0-28.0). The measured PaCO2 is 27.3 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 135 - (96 + 12) = 27.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old man in ED with confusion and blurred vision after a possible ingestion from a solvent container at home. He’s tachypnoeic on arrival, with no clear history from the scene.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.learning_objective: Recognise toxic alcohol ingestion as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 8 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The clinical context and only modest lactate of 1.4 mmol/L support toxic alcohol ingestion over isolated lactic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 20.0 mmHg (acceptable range 18.0-22.0). The measured PaCO2 is 20.5 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (93 + 8) = 39.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Toxic alcohol ingestion can cause a severe raised anion gap metabolic acidosis due to accumulation of acidic metabolites. Common agents include methanol and ethylene glycol. The degree of acidosis is often disproportionate to any measured lactate, and may worsen rapidly as metabolism progresses. The key clinical clue is an unexplained high anion gap acidosis without a clear metabolic cause. This is a time-critical diagnosis, and early recognition is essential to prevent ongoing toxicity and organ injury.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: An unexplained severe high anion gap metabolic acidosis — especially if disproportionate to lactate, should prompt urgent consideration of toxic alcohol ingestion.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 35M in ED with several days of persistent vomiting and retching, now tired and mildly dehydrated on arrival. No diarrhoea or fever reported.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 37 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Metabolic alkalosis compensation, the expected PaCO2 is about 49.1 mmHg (acceptable range 46.1-52.1). The measured PaCO2 is 49.0 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, leading to a metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and increased pH. The body compensates by hypoventilating to retain CO₂, raising PaCO₂. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help maintain the alkalosis, so recognising this pattern is important as correction requires replacement of both volume and chloride.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 31F presents to ED with nausea and vomiting after a recent alcohol binge. She reports poor oral intake over the past couple of days and has had epigastric discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 16 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 32.0 mmHg (acceptable range 30.0-34.0). The measured PaCO2 is 31.3 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 133 - (99 + 16) = 18.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.42, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 23.8 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 20.8 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.8-22.8). The measured HCO3 is 15.0 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 138 - (102 + 15.0) = 21.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Here the bicarbonate is 15.0 mmol/L, lower than the roughly 20.8 mmol/L expected for isolated respiratory alkalosis. With an anion gap of 21.0, that extra drop is best explained by a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis. In salicylate toxicity this is a true mixed primary disorder, not just a compensatory response.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is characteristic of salicylate toxicity, where early hyperventilation is followed by metabolic acidosis. The combination should prompt urgent consideration of overdose, even if the history is unclear. Early recognition is critical, as clinical deterioration can be rapid.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate cases are high-risk because the alkalosis and acidosis co-exist, so compensation formulas help expose the mixed picture quickly.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 19F with type 1 diabetes comes in after missing insulin for a day or two, with polyuria and deep breathing noted by family. She looks uncomfortable but is answering questions appropriately.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 15 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 18.8 mmol/L, which strongly supports DKA as the cause of this high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 30.5 mmHg (acceptable range 28.5-32.5). The measured PaCO2 is 30.3 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (102 + 15) = 22.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Insulin deficiency leads to unchecked lipolysis and ketone production, resulting in a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Marked hyperglycaemia is a key feature, distinguishing DKA from other causes of ketoacidosis. The acidosis is often more severe than in starvation or alcoholic ketoacidosis, and may be accompanied by dehydration and electrolyte disturbances.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with marked hyperglycaemia suggests DKA, but always consider an additional metabolic process if compensation is inappropriate or the bicarbonate is not as expected.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 70-year-old woman in ED with 2 days of loose stools. She looks dehydrated with dry mucous membranes.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss, leading to a metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 27.5 mmHg (acceptable range 25.5-29.5). The measured PaCO2 is 26.4 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (111 + 13) = 12.0, which is normal.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diarrhoea causes a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis through direct loss of bicarbonate from the GI tract. This results in a hyperchloraemic acidosis as chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality. The pattern is typically accompanied by volume depletion and may be associated with low potassium. Recognising this mechanism helps distinguish it from renal causes of bicarbonate loss and from high anion gap acidoses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: The acidosis fits a normal anion gap pattern, but think bicarbonate loss from the gut rather than unmeasured acids.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory alkalosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory alkalosis

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.43, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 20.1 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 20.0 mmol/L (acceptable range 18.0-22.0). The measured HCO3 is 12.8 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 143 - (109 + 12.8) = 21.2, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: For this degree of respiratory alkalosis, the bicarbonate would be expected to be about 20.0 mmol/L, but it is 12.8 mmol/L here. That extra fall of about 7.2 mmol/L is too large for compensation alone, and with an anion gap of 21.2 it points to an added high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern reflects significant underlying illness with two concurrent processes. A normal or near-normal pH should not reassure, as a serious metabolic acidosis may be masked by hyperventilation. This commonly occurs in conditions such as sepsis, where early respiratory changes precede clinical deterioration.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: If a low PaCO2 does not fully explain the acid-base picture, use the anion gap to uncover an added metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 28 year old male presents with acute severe flank pain from suspected renal colic. He is restless at the bedside and is in significant pain. A blood gas was taken.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 28 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits acute hyperventilation such as panic.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Hyperventilation increases alveolar ventilation, leading to excessive loss of CO₂. As PaCO₂ falls, hydrogen ion concentration decreases, causing a rise in pH and a primary respiratory alkalosis. This disturbance is driven by increased breathing rather than a metabolic process, with common triggers including anxiety or panic, pain, hypoxia, and systemic illness such as sepsis.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)
cases.clinical_stem: 65M brought to ED with hypotension and tachycardia, drowsy but arousable. Cool peripheries and brief notes from triage suggest shock.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 13.9 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactate is 9.4 mmol/L, which supports lactic acidosis in the septic clinical context.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 28.9 mmHg (acceptable range 26.9-30.9). The measured PaCO2 is 30.7 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 144 - (95 + 13.9) = 35.1, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Lactic acidosis results from impaired tissue oxygen delivery or utilisation, leading to accumulation of lactate and a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Common causes include septic or cardiogenic shock, severe anaemia, or regional ischaemia. The elevated lactate reflects underlying hypoperfusion or cellular dysfunction. This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A raised lactate explains the gap, but the urgent job is still to find and reverse the cause of hypoperfusion or tissue stress.

This is a marker of significant physiological stress, and urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause is critical. cases.title: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent HAGMA
cases.clinical_stem: 75-year-old man brought to ED with worsening breathlessness and increasing drowsiness over the day. He’s working hard to breathe, using accessory muscles, and is more difficult to rouse than earlier.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

options: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

answer_key.final_diagnosis: Respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise respiratory acidosis with a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis when bicarbonate is too low for isolated respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 71.5 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The overall pattern is respiratory acidosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In chronic respiratory acidosis, the kidneys retain HCO3 to partially offset the raised PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Chronic respiratory acidosis, the expected HCO3 is about 36.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 34.6-38.6). The measured HCO3 is 25.3 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 142 - (93 + 25.3) = 23.7, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Respiratory acidosis should produce a compensatory rise in bicarbonate to about 36.6 mmol/L, but the measured bicarbonate is only 25.3 mmol/L. That shortfall of about 11.3 mmol/L, together with an anion gap of 23.7, supports an additional high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern suggests severe physiological compromise, with both impaired ventilation and a concurrent metabolic acidosis. It is often seen in critically unwell patients and indicates a high risk of deterioration. Prompt assessment and escalation of care are essential.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When respiratory acidosis and a raised anion gap coexist, expected compensation helps confirm that two primary processes are present.

cases.title: Starvation ketosis (subtle HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 58M in ED with 3/7 of poor oral intake and weight loss. He has felt fatigued, with some nausea and occasional vomiting.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise starvation ketosis as a subtle high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and normal-range glucose

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 17 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The glucose is 4.8 mmol/L, which is not markedly elevated. This is more in keeping with starvation ketosis over DKA.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 33.5 mmHg (acceptable range 31.5-35.5). The measured PaCO2 is 33.6 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 139 - (104 + 17) = 18.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is typically milder than DKA because some endogenous insulin activity remains, limiting unchecked ketogenesis. The key clinical clue is a history of reduced intake with normal or only mildly elevated glucose. This pattern should prompt correction of the fasting state and assessment for the underlying reason the patient has not been eating.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: When ketosis occurs without major hyperglycaemia, use the clinical context to separate starvation ketosis from DKA.

cases.title: Alcoholic ketoacidosis (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 31M in ED after a recent alcohol binge, with reduced oral intake over the past 3 days. He looks dry and has been tachypnoeic, with an ABG taken on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise alcoholic ketoacidosis as a high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 16 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: The history pattern and the high anion gap metabolic acidosis fit alcoholic ketoacidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic acidosis, the body compensates by hyperventilating to lower PaCO2. Using Winter's formula, the expected PaCO2 is about 32.0 mmHg (acceptable range 30.0-34.0). The measured PaCO2 is 32.9 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 136 - (100 + 16) = 20.0, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Increased ketone production in the setting of heavy alcohol use and poor oral intake can cause a raised anion gap metabolic acidosis. Glucose is typically normal or low due to impaired gluconeogenesis, helping distinguish this from DKA. Vomiting may contribute a concurrent metabolic alkalosis, masking the degree of acidaemia. The key clue is recent alcohol use with poor intake, and management focuses on reversing the metabolic state and addressing the underlying cause.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: A high anion gap acidosis without marked hyperglycaemia should keep alcoholic ketoacidosis high on the differential list.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: ED handover for a 50-year-old man with a productive cough and fever, now more breathless and tachypnoeic. He looks unwell with a fast pulse and low-grade confusion, and an ABG was taken on arrival.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 27.8 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits sepsis-related hyperventilation without an added metabolic process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 21.6 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.6-23.6). The measured HCO3 is 21.9 mmol/L. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate metabolic compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Sepsis increases respiratory drive through inflammatory and metabolic signals, leading to hyperventilation and a fall in PaCO₂. This respiratory alkalosis often occurs early in the disease process before metabolic abnormalities develop.

cases.title: Simple normal anion gap metabolic acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: A 24 year old female who recently returned from Bali presents with several days of profuse diarrhoea. She is clinically dry with a mild tachycardia, but is otherwise well.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise a simple bicarbonate-driven metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 14 mmol/L, which is low and supports a primary metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is consistent with gastrointestinal bicarbonate loss, leading to a metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Metabolic acidosis can occur when bicarbonate is lost from the body or when the kidneys are unable to excrete acid effectively. As bicarbonate falls, chloride rises to maintain electroneutrality, which is why the chloride may be elevated. A common cause of this pattern is bicarbonate loss through the gastrointestinal tract, such as with diarrhoea.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: A 40 year old male presents with reduced consciousness after taking prescribed opioids with alcohol. His is bradypnoeic with minimal respiratory effort. A blood gas is taken.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is < 7.35, consistent with an acidaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 71 mmHg, which is elevated and supports a primary respiratory acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits opioid-related hypoventilation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Opioid toxicity causes respiratory depression, leading to reduced ventilation and retention of CO₂. This results in a respiratory acidosis with an elevated PaCO₂ and a fall in pH. The key problem is inadequate ventilation, and early recognition is important as this is a reversible cause of respiratory failure.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 27M brought to ED after taking a large amount of aspirin. He’s tachypnoeic and sweaty, with ringing in his ears and some nausea.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

options: Respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the classic respiratory alkalosis plus high anion gap metabolic acidosis pattern of salicylate toxicity

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is 7.42, which is near normal - do not let a near-normal pH hide an underlying process.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: PaCO2 is 25.3 mmHg, which is low and supports a primary respiratory alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This is the classic salicylate pattern: respiratory alkalosis with concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In respiratory alkalosis, buffering and early renal compensation lower HCO3 to partially offset the low PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Acute respiratory alkalosis, the expected HCO3 is about 21.1 mmol/L (acceptable range 19.1-23.1). The measured HCO3 is 15.8 mmol/L. This mismatch does not fit a simple single-process compensation pattern and suggests a mixed disorder rather than isolated compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Anion Gap Analysis
explanation_blueprint.body: The Anion gap is 140 - (100 + 15.8) = 24.2, which is raised. This suggests the presence of unmeasured anions - most commonly lactate, ketones, or toxins.

explanation_blueprint.title: Additional Metabolic Process
explanation_blueprint.body: Here the bicarbonate is 15.8 mmol/L, lower than the roughly 21.1 mmol/L expected for isolated respiratory alkalosis. With an anion gap of 24.2, that extra drop is best explained by a concurrent high anion gap metabolic acidosis. In salicylate toxicity this is a true mixed primary disorder, not just a compensatory response.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern is characteristic of salicylate toxicity, where early hyperventilation is followed by metabolic acidosis. The combination should prompt urgent consideration of overdose, even if the history is unclear. Early recognition is critical, as clinical deterioration can be rapid.

explanation_blueprint.title: Key Takeaway
explanation_blueprint.body: Salicylate cases are high-risk because the alkalosis and acidosis co-exist, so compensation formulas help expose the mixed picture quickly.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old woman in ED with persistent vomiting and retching since yesterday. She looks dry with reduced oral intake and is mildly dizzy on standing.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 32 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from gastric losses.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Metabolic alkalosis compensation, the expected PaCO2 is about 45.6 mmHg (acceptable range 42.6-48.6). The measured PaCO2 is 44.3 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Vomiting causes loss of gastric acid, leading to a metabolic alkalosis with elevated bicarbonate and increased pH. The body compensates by hypoventilating to retain CO₂, raising PaCO₂. Ongoing chloride and volume depletion help maintain the alkalosis, so recognising this pattern is important as correction requires replacement of both volume and chloride.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 81M brought to ED with a few days of dizziness and dry mouth after a recent thiazide increase. He looks a bit dehydrated on exam.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion

explanation_blueprint.title: pH Status
explanation_blueprint.body: The pH is > 7.45, consistent with an alkalaemia.

explanation_blueprint.title: Primary Disorder
explanation_blueprint.body: HCO3 is 32 mmol/L, which is elevated and supports a primary metabolic alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Diagnosis
explanation_blueprint.body: This pattern fits chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis. Diuretic use causes volume depletion and chloride loss, which maintains the alkalosis.

explanation_blueprint.title: Compensation
explanation_blueprint.body: In metabolic alkalosis, the body compensates by hypoventilating to retain PaCO2. Using the expected compensation rule for Metabolic alkalosis compensation, the expected PaCO2 is about 45.6 mmHg (acceptable range 42.6-48.6). The measured PaCO2 is 44.4 mmHg. This is within the expected range, indicating appropriate respiratory compensation.

explanation_blueprint.title: Clinical Significance
explanation_blueprint.body: Diuretic use can cause metabolic alkalosis through volume depletion and renal loss of chloride, leading to increased bicarbonate reabsorption. This results in a chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis, often with low or low-normal potassium. The alkalosis is maintained by ongoing volume and chloride depletion, even after the initial trigger. Recognising this pattern is important, as correction of volume status and chloride deficit is key to reversing the disturbance.
